Content_Sketch: Handshake in European cafe - warm smile across the table, coffee, cobblestone outside the window. You know their real name. They do not know yours.
VO_Draft_EN: They trust you. That is how you win. The man across the table is laughing at something you said. His hand is warm when he shakes yours. He thinks you are a consultant from Munich. He thinks you went to the same university. He thinks you like the same wine. None of it is true. But he believes all of it. You know his real name. His home address. His children's school. He does not know yours. He does not know your country. He does not know that every word he says to you goes into a file he will never see.

Content_Sketch: Weight statement: you have never told anyone who you are. Not even the person sitting next to you every day.
VO_Draft_EN: You have never told anyone who you are. Not your neighbors in Vienna. Not the woman you date as cover. Not even the man who sits beside you on the plane back to Tel Aviv. Your name changes with the passport. Your accent changes with the city. Your life changes with the briefing. The only thing that stays the same is the watch on your left wrist. No brand. No markings. Just time. That is all you are allowed to keep.

Content_Sketch: Age 21. Elite IDF unit. You are noticed by someone you never see. A phone call with no organization name.
VO_Draft_EN: You are twenty-one. You have spent three years in an IDF reconnaissance unit. You are good at things most people never learn. Reading terrain. Reading people. Disappearing. One afternoon a phone rings in the barracks. A voice you do not recognize asks if you are available for a meeting. No organization name. No details. Just an address in Tel Aviv and a time.

Content_Sketch: Months of psychological screening. Simulations under assumed identities. Stress tests that strip you down.
VO_Draft_EN: The screening lasts five months. They put you in rooms with strangers and tell you to become someone else. They give you a name, a backstory, a childhood you never had. You walk into a cafe in Haifa and order coffee as a man named Daniel. They watch. They test whether you flinch when someone calls a name that is not yours. They test whether you can lie to someone's face while they are being kind to you. Most people fail. You do not.

Content_Sketch: Midrasha academy near Herzliya. Two years. Surveillance detection, source recruitment, cover management.
VO_Draft_EN: The Midrasha. Near Herzliya. Two years. They teach you how to spot a tail on a crowded street. How to recruit a source who does not want to be recruited. How to build a cover identity that holds under pressure. You practice dead drops in parking garages. You memorize faces from photographs that are destroyed after you see them. You learn three languages. You forget which accent belongs to which name. This is the part they do not put in the movies. It is not exciting. It is repetitive. It is designed to make lying feel like breathing.

Content_Sketch: Final exercise: three weeks in a foreign city under a fake name. Graduation with no diploma.
VO_Draft_EN: Final exercise. Three weeks in Prague under a name you chose yourself. You rent an apartment. You build a social circle. You eat dinner with people who believe every word you say. By the end of the third week, you respond to your fake name faster than your real one. Something shifts. You do not notice it yet. You graduate. There is no ceremony. No diploma. No photograph. Nothing proves you were ever here. Your instructor shakes your hand and says one sentence. Do not forget who you are.

Content_Sketch: Deployed to a European capital. New identity: businessman. Apartment with someone else's name.
VO_Draft_EN: Vienna. Your name is Martin Kessler. You are a technology consultant. You have business cards with a phone number that routes to a desk in Tel Aviv. Your apartment is leased under a company that does not exist. You drink coffee at the same cafe every morning. You tip well. You smile at the barista. You are building a pattern. Not yours. Martin's.

Content_Sketch: First target: build trust brick by brick. Coffee then dinner then birthdays.
VO_Draft_EN: Your first target is a man named Friedrich. He works at a research institute with access to things your country needs to know. You meet him at a conference. You buy him a drink. You ask about his work. He is lonely. Most targets are. Over the next four months you become his closest friend. Coffee every Thursday. Dinner once a month. His birthday, you bring a bottle of wine he mentioned once in passing. He is moved by this. He should not be. You remembered because it was in the file.

Content_Sketch: Target shares private things. You file every word into a classified report.
VO_Draft_EN: Friedrich tells you about his daughter's illness. He tells you about his failing marriage. He tells you about the project at work he is not supposed to discuss. You listen. You nod. You say the right things. Later, in the safe house, you type every word into a report. His daughter's diagnosis. His wife's name. The project details. You mark it CLASSIFIED and send it to Tel Aviv. You wash your hands and go to bed. You do not know why you wash your hands. You just do.

Content_Sketch: Phone call home to Israel. Your daughter asks when Abba is coming home.
VO_Draft_EN: You call home on Thursdays. Your wife's voice sounds different each time. Further. Your daughter picks up once. Abba, when are you coming home? Soon, motek. You have said soon for eleven months. She tells you about a drawing she made at school. A family. Four stick figures. You are the smallest one. She says it is because you are far away. You hang up and sit in Martin Kessler's apartment in Martin Kessler's chair. The watch on your wrist ticks. You are not sure whose time it is keeping.

Content_Sketch: Sayanim network. Shadow infrastructure. You live in a world built on loyalty you cannot repay.
VO_Draft_EN: A man you have never met leaves a car in a parking garage with the keys under the mat. A dentist in the third district treats your tooth under a name that does not match your face. A woman rents an apartment for you and never asks a single question. They are called sayanim. Helpers. They do it because they believe in something larger than you. You use their loyalty like fuel. You never say thank you. You are not allowed to.

Content_Sketch: Target invites you to dinner at his home. His daughter is the same age as yours.
VO_Draft_EN: Friedrich invites you to dinner. At his home. You have never been inside. His wife makes schnitzel. His daughter sets the table. She is four. Same age as yours. She asks if you want to see her room. You say yes. There are drawings on the wall. Stick figures. A family. You stand there for three seconds too long. Friedrich calls from the kitchen. Martin, come, the wine is open.

Content_Sketch: Phone buzzes. Code: Activate. You keep smiling. You pour more wine.
VO_Draft_EN: Your phone buzzes in your jacket pocket. You excuse yourself to the bathroom. One word on the screen. Activate. You look at yourself in the mirror. You flush the toilet for the sound. You walk back to the table. You sit down. You pour yourself more wine. You laugh at Friedrich's joke about his neighbor. You compliment the food. Nobody at the table notices anything. Your hand is steady. Your voice is steady. Everything is steady.

Content_Sketch: You could refuse. No gun to your head. You choose to continue.
VO_Draft_EN: You could refuse. You could stand up right now and say Friedrich, my name is not Martin. I work for a foreign intelligence service. Everything I told you is a lie. Run. Nobody is holding a gun to your head. There is no immediate threat. This is not a movie. This is a dining room with drawings on the wall and a child's laughter in the next room. And you choose to stay. You choose to smile. You choose to finish the wine.

Content_Sketch: You extract what you need. You say goodnight. Next morning the apartment is empty. Two weeks later you read the news.
VO_Draft_EN: You steer the conversation to the project. Friedrich talks freely. He trusts you. He has no reason not to. You remember every detail. You say goodnight at the door. His wife hugs you. His daughter waves from the window. You walk to your car and do not look back. The next morning your apartment is empty. Your phone number is disconnected. Martin Kessler no longer exists. Two weeks later you read a short article in a German newspaper. Friedrich has been arrested. No details. No trial date. His daughter's drawings are still on the wall of an empty house.

Content_Sketch: Next mission then the next. Faces blur. Only the wristwatch stays the same.
VO_Draft_EN: Next city. Next name. Next target. You stop counting after the seventh. The faces change but the pattern does not. Meet. Build trust. Extract. Disappear. You get better at it. The lying becomes smoother. The exits become cleaner. The only thing that does not change is the watch on your wrist. Same one since the academy. No brand. No markings. Just time passing.

Content_Sketch: Back in Israel. Daughter runs to mother. Wife says she does not know you.
VO_Draft_EN: Between assignments you go home to Israel. Your daughter is six now. She runs to her mother when you walk through the door. Your wife looks at you the way you look at a stranger on a train. Familiar. But not known. She says it one night. I do not know who you are anymore. You want to say that makes two of us. You do not say that. You say nothing. You stand in the kitchen for a while, looking at the tiles.

Content_Sketch: Leave Mossad. No ceremony. No medal. Someone calls a name and you flinch.
VO_Draft_EN: You leave. Not dramatically. You just stop going. The paperwork takes a week. There is no ceremony. No medal. No retirement speech. Your handler shakes your hand and says be well. You sit in a cafe in Tel Aviv. A stranger behind you says the name Martin. You flinch. Then you realize he is talking to someone else. You look at your hands. They are shaking. You hold the coffee cup tighter. You do not know whose hands these are.

Content_Sketch: Therapist cannot be told. Google shows target's family photos. Close the laptop.
VO_Draft_EN: The therapist asks about your work history. You say government consulting. She asks if anything traumatic happened. You sit there. You cannot tell her. Not because you do not want to. Because it is illegal. You Google Friedrich once. His profile is still there. Family photos. His daughter at a birthday party. She looks like yours. You stare at the screen. Then you close the laptop. You wash your hands. You do not know why you keep doing that.

Content_Sketch: Another cafe. No mission. Your hands still scan exits by reflex.
VO_Draft_EN: Another cafe. Another table. Another person across from you. But this time no file. No handler. No extraction plan. Just coffee. You look at the exits out of habit. You count the people near the door. You notice the man two tables over checked his phone three times in two minutes. You catch yourself. There is no mission. There is no one watching. But your hands are still scanning. Your eyes are still reading.

Content_Sketch: You lived two lives. And now you do not know which one was real.
VO_Draft_EN: You look down at your wrist. The watch is still there. Same one. No brand. No markings. The only honest thing you ever carried. You lived two lives for a country that will never say your name. You built trust for a living. And now you sit alone in a cafe with hands that cannot stop reading the room. You do not know which life was real. You are not sure it matters. If you ever wondered what it costs to make someone believe in you. Now you know.
